They Don't Take Just Any Mutt
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Parks Commissioner Henry J. Stern's solution to the perennial problem of leashless dogs in Central Park is the neighborhood dog run. Evidently, he has never tried to join one. In New York, it takes an endless wait and a sizable check to set your dogs free. 

     For years on our 6 A.M. walk, my husband, David, and I wrestled with our golden retrievers, Willi and Dune, as they feasted on the gourmet garbage from our SoHo neighborhood: seared tuna from Zoe, Thai noodles from Penang and focaccia from Dean & DeLuca. Tired of this tug-of-war, we decided to sign up for the Mercer-Houston Dog Run, a fenced slab of concrete adjacent to New York University that contains a plastic swimming pool and four park benches. Willi and Dune would retrieve tennis balls while we peacefully sipped coffee and read  the paper. No more morning battle of wills, no more snacking on trash.

Congress's Knee-Jerk Helium Giveaway
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For 35 years, the Government has kept a vast reserve of helium, which when cooled is one of the earth's most technologically vital fluids. Yet from the casual manner in which the House voted overwhelmingly last week to sell the reserve to the private sector, one might have thought it was dispensing with laughing gas. 

     The Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee is to take up a similar bill next week. Industry approves of the plan. Scientists, however, are rightly  worried.

F.B.I. Says It Reviewed a Computer Service for Indecency
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The Justice Department came under fire today from a Federal judge at closing  arguments on the constitutionality of the Communications Decency Act, after it was disclosed that the Federal Bureau of Investigation had reviewed complaints of indecent material on Compuserve. 

     The Government had agreed to refrain from investigating complaints about on-line indecency while a challenge to the new law was being decided in Federal  court. A three-judge panel is hearing the case, which is proceeding under special rules established by the statute itself to insure swift judicial review.

wiki_events.headline: After the aircraft's departure from Miami, a fire started by improperly handled chemical oxygen generators in the cargo hold of Atlanta-bound ValuJet Airlines Flight 592 causes the Douglas DC-9 to crash in the Florida Everglades, killing all 110 on board.

pages.title: ValuJet Flight 592
pages.abstract: ValuJet Flight 592 was a regularly scheduled flight from Miami to Atlanta in the United States. On May 11, 1996, the ValuJet Airlines McDonnell Douglas DC-9 operating the route crashed into the Florida Everglades about 10 minutes after departing Miami due to a fire in the cargo compartment. The fire was caused by mislabeled and improperly stored chemical oxygen generators. All 110 people on board were killed.

Idyll By Blue Waters
nytimes_articles.abstract:
BLESSED with a benign Mediterranean microclimate, spectacular mountain scenery and pellucid waters, Lake Garda was already a favorite resort in Roman times. With an area of almost 143 square miles, it is Italy's largest lake, and  its steady breezes make it ideal for sailing and windsurfing. However, even if you don't have (or rent) your own boat, between the end of March and the end of  September you can easily get around on the water on the network of public ferries. 

     Spring and fall are the best times to visit, since Garda's towns and villages can be crowded at the height of summer, and Christine, my wife, and I decide on  a spring trip this year to explore the southern part of the lake, with the ultimate goal of seeing one of Garda's most unusual but often overlooked attractions: Il Vittoriale, the extraordinary house and hillside estate at Gardone Riviera, created by the writer, aviator and military adventurer Gabriele D'Annunzio, who lived there between 1921 and his death in 1938.

Flower Childhood
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In 1969, my father was arrested for his part in organizing an antiwar protest in Boston and was sentenced to a two-year prison term. (He and my mother had split up a few years earlier, but they had remained close, sharing the child-rearing duties and trying to forge a new kind of divorce, one that was in  keeping with their progressive politics.) It would take me many ywars before I would understand the times in which this event took place. I'm still trying, and failing, to understand. But before I knew anything of politics, or war, or social movements, I knew my father's enveloping presence. He was more than six feet tall, with huge hands and a laugh that rippled through his chest when he carried me. He crooned me to sleep, twirled me in his arms, and then -- without  warning -- he was gone. 

     My father began serving his time not long after his 28th birthday. I was 3 years old. Shortly after he went to prison, my mother took me to see him. He had written her a letter asking for books and a new pair of tennis shoes; he was playing a lot of pickup basketball in the yard to keep his head clear. On the ride out to the prison, I clutched a box of black Converse high-tops in my lap,  my head bubbling with important things to tell him.

nytimes_articles.headline: Hong Kong Before The British
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A NARROW causeway leads away from the village of Tai O on Lantau Island across a shallow green bay to the temple of Hua Wong. There, on a terrace overlooking the water, feathery tamarisk trees shelter a pair of stone benches,  creating thin shade from the pale spring sunlight in front of the cluster of gray, stuccoed halls, each with its own tiled roof and roof ridge alive with glazed ceramic dragons, lions, fish and phoenixes. Water laps at the seawall; a scruffy orange cat sleeps on the sun-warmed stone of the temple loggia. 

     The temple, on the west coast of Lantau Island -- the largest of Hong Kong's  islands -- was built in 1699 to honor Yang Liang-jie, the uncle and guardian of  a Song child-emperor who fled the Mongols in 1277 and died in Canton at the age  of 9. Inside, the yellow flames of oil lamps cast a gold light on the altar, which is laden with bowls of oranges and decorated with attenuated figures. In a side room, a kiln for burning paper offerings is built of brick in the form of a pagoda. Signs of age, use and faith and a deep sense of peace are everywhere.

nytimes_articles.headline: Flower Childhood
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In 1969, my father was arrested for his part in organizing an antiwar protest in Boston and was sentenced to a two-year prison term. (He and my mother had split up a few years earlier, but they had remained close, sharing the child-rearing duties and trying to forge a new kind of divorce, one that was in  keeping with their progressive politics.) It would take me many ywars before I would understand the times in which this event took place. I'm still trying, and failing, to understand. But before I knew anything of politics, or war, or social movements, I knew my father's enveloping presence. He was more than six feet tall, with huge hands and a laugh that rippled through his chest when he carried me. He crooned me to sleep, twirled me in his arms, and then -- without  warning -- he was gone. 

     My father began serving his time not long after his 28th birthday. I was 3 years old. Shortly after he went to prison, my mother took me to see him. He had written her a letter asking for books and a new pair of tennis shoes; he was playing a lot of pickup basketball in the yard to keep his head clear. SOAPBOX;Caught in the Middle School Search
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Getting our daughter into a new school for sixth grade seems as nerve-wracking as applying for college. Since early October, hardly a week has passed without some effort or concern about her future. Research the options, then make school visits, file applications by deadlines, take tests and wait. I'm not writing about admissions to selective private schools. Like us, thousands of New York City families live through a grueling process of finding a public middle school for their children. 

     Middle school -- covering grades 6 to 8 -- is replacing New York's old junior highs that contained grades 7 through 9. Many public elementary schools still offer a sixth grade, but elementary education is evolving toward kindergarten through fifth.

nytimes_articles.headline: Q. & A.
EARNING IT;Who's Reading Your E-Mail? Maybe the Boss
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN Michael A. Smyth sent an E-mail message to a co-worker in 1994 calling his employers "back-stabbing bastards," he had little concern that someone else  might be peeking at what he thought was a private message. After all, he contended that the company had promised him that his E-mail communications would remain confidential. 

     They weren't. Executives at his company, Pillsbury, saw the message and others like it, and Mr. Smyth was dismissed for what the company deemed "inappropriate and unprofessional comments." The company said it had never told  Mr. Smyth that his E-mail messages would be private.

Florida's Feast of Tiffany
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Near Orlando, the gracious old town of Winter Park is home to a treasury of glowing glass. 

      THE map of central Florida provided by the car-rental people shows the city  of Orlando in bright yellow, an irregular polyhedron that seems to head in all directions at once. I am looking for Winter Park, and there it is: a small, unassertive pastel patch, in the upper right-hand area, engulfed, as if it were  simply a neighborhood, a small part of the large golden whole.

nytimes_articles.headline: Living on Mother's Prayer
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In my play "Seven Guitars," near the end of the second act as the characters  are preparing to go to the Mother's Day dance at the local blues club, Red Carter is offered his choice of a red or white carnation that Vera has made out  of crepe paper. He replies: "I need me a red flower. My mother's still living. Even as I know it got to come to the day I wear a white flower, I hope it ain't  no time soon." 

     When I was a child growing up in Pittsburgh during the 1950's, Mother's Day was rivaled only by Easter as an occasion to sport your finest haberdashery. Like Easter, it was also an obligatory church day, and as my sisters and I would leave the house we would each be wearing a red crepe paper carnation made by a family friend, Baby Dear, who would sell them on the street corners and in the local bars and taverns, as it was the custom to wear either a red or white carnation -- red if your mother was alive and white if she was dead.

nytimes_articles.headline: Clinton Says Maternity Plans Need to Offer 2 Hospital Days
Science Fiction
WEDDINGS;Tara A. McCarthy, Patrick T. Koeppl
The Great Mutiny
Uncle Chul Gets Rich
nytimes_articles.abstract:
My father's youngest brother, Uncle Chul, shared the Lees' famously bad reaction to liquor, which was to turn beet-red in the face, grow dizzy and finally get sick. In spite of this, he was always happy to stay up late at family gatherings. After a few Scotches he would really loosen up, and, with the notable exception of my mother, we all appreciated his rough language and racy stories. Only when Mother came in from the kitchen would his talk soften, for he knew he had always fallen short in her eyes. If they were ever alone together, say in the kitchen, after dinner, he would use the most decorous voice in asking for a glass or a fresh bucket of ice, and even offer to help load the  dishwasher or run an errand to the store. 

     On one of those nights we sped off, both happy for a break in the long evening. He asked me about school, what sports I was playing, but the conversation inevitably turned toward my parents, and particularly my mother --  how much she had invested in me, that I was her great hope. I thought it was odd that he was speaking this way, like my other relatives, and I answered with some criticism of her -- that she was too anxious and overbearing. He stared at me and, with a hard solemnity I had not heard from him before, said that my mother  was one of the finest people one could ever know. He kept a grip on the wheel and in the ensuing quiet of the drive I could sense how he must have both admired and despised her. In many respects, my mother was an unrelenting woman.  She tended to measure people by the mark of a few principles of conduct: ask no  help from anyone, always plan for the long run and practice (her own variation of) the golden rule, which was to treat others much better than oneself.

Granada, Andalusia's Heart and Soul
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Pomegranate, the woman said. 

     It was blustery in the late winter, and I was approaching Granada from the north, down the spine of the country. I had never been in Andalusia, and my anticipation was high. The strains of "Bolero" had wafted in my brain for days.  In my mind's eye was a sunlit city on a hill, a city of gleaming white and red tiles, and beyond a snowcapped range of mountains against a deep blue sky. In the middle distance, on another hill, between the city and the mountains, was a  great red castle of incomparable beauty and majesty. This was, of course, the Calat-alhamra of the Moorish kings of Spain, the Alhambra.

EARNING IT;A Study Links Computers to Higher Pay
nytimes_articles.abstract:
COMPUTERS, it turns out, provide a good reason for employees and their bosses to get along at work. A study by two professors at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania has found that pay goes up for each group when the other's use of computers increases. 

     Adjusting for factors like the educational background of the work force, the  professors found that the earnings of production workers increased 3 percent annually when their managers' use of computers doubled. Only half of the increase is attributable to greater skill on the part of the workers.

Small Victories
Zambia: In Africa, Up Close and Personal
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When I toured Keny and Tanzania in the 1980's, I found peerless viewing - except for the gridlocked hordes of my own species surrounding every lion. Last  June, when I spent a little more than two weeks farther down the Great Rift Valley in Zambia, the game far outnumbered us gapers. On one unforgettable, five-day streach, my companion, Emily Fisher, and I didn't encounter a single other tourist. Here, in south central Africa, we could still track spoor untrampled by Toyota treads and fancy ourselves back in the pristine bush of Teddy Roosevelt or Ernest Hemingway. Indeed, Papa's 29-year-old grandson Sean was apprenticing there as a guide. 

      Bypassing Lusaka, the capital, we landed in Zambia at Mfuwe, the entry airport to our trekking grounds, the vast (nearly 3,500 square miles) South Luangwa National Par. Our original base was a few miles east of the park at the  Kapani Safari Lodge, a row of cottages with verandas overlooking a lagoon brimming with waterfowl, hippopotamuses and crocodiles. We arrived in time for lunch on the open dining porch with Sean Hemingway and Norman Carr, the creator  of the walking safari. Carr had come to this area as an elephant control officer in 1939, when it was Northern Rhodesia. And he is today, at 83, the owner of the lodge and master teacher of a new generation of guides. Plotting our adventure,  I had read Carr's book, "Valley of the Elephants," a missionary work for this region and for safariing on foot. "I realized what is lost to people nowadays who rush around national parks in Land-Rovers and zebra-striped minibuses," he wrote. "To my mind, the only way to capture the true atmosphere of Africa is by  being part of it, observing it peacefully at a leisurely pace away from the noise and smell of gasoline."

VIEWS;For 63 Years, He's Delivered
nytimes_articles.abstract:
At 2:30 A.M., James Capobianco is already on his way to the Newark Farmers Market, ready to buy today's fresh produce. As he passes Newark Airport, he hears a familiar, reassuring sound: the roar of jet engines. 

     When Mr. Capobianco, 80, started this route in 1933, there were no jets at Newark. Franklin D. Roosevelt had just been sworn in as President. And there were many others who did what the 17-year-old Jimmy did, driving their produce trucks door to door, selling fresh fruit and flowers to the housewives of Essex  County.

Op-Ed Hothead?
Blue Sky, California
nytimes_articles.abstract:
My father and I are together in the sky. I am 8 and he has taken me up in the Piper Cherokee, a four-seat, single-prop, family sedan of an airplane he rents once in a while. My father's idea is that we will fly over our house and get a look at it from a new vantage point, from high above the Santa Clara Valley on a clear summer morning. We will fly over at an agreed-upon time and my mother will stand in our backyard and wave. 

     "Won't be long till we get there. Five, ten minutes. Have a look." My father's voice in my headphones cuts through the vibrating thrum of the airplane's engine. He smiles and traces a circle on the window with the tip of his index finger, as if marking an intersection on a road map. I follow his finger, look down and suddenly, vaguely, I am terrified.

Books in Brief;A Long Way From Manila
nytimes_articles.abstract: Where are you from?" is a question too often asked of the young, American-born Filipino women who populate M. Evelina Galang's stirring debut collection of stories, HER WILD AMERICAN SELF (Coffee House Press, paper, $12.95). Whether the person asking is a bystander at a bus stop, a school classmate wrinkling her nose at the smell of a box lunch of rice and fish, or a  film director known for his sleazy attitude toward Asian women, none are satisfied when the answer turns out to be Michigan, Wisconsin or New York. But if the ignorance and insensitivity of strangers is borne with graceful yet sarcastic humor, the pressures of home -- under the zealous gaze of parents eager for their daughters to partake of the "land of opportunity" -- yield a frustrated, rebellious sadness. In "Miss Teenage Sampaguita," a visiting aunt observes her brother's tyrannical hold on her niece, who is expected to become a doctor and is deprived of everyday pleasures like the right to date or even talk on the telephone. And in "Talk to Me, Milagros," a silent child watches as her lawyer father, recently arrived from the Philippines, is reduced to working as a busboy in a diner. While a number of Ms. Galang's stories lack a certain dynamic force, all are told in an elegant, mesmerizing style. The finest, "Filming Sausage," is sharp with tension, while the brief, chantlike monologues that frame the collection are as lyrical as prayers.   Abby Frucht

Beirut Revisited: A Traveler's Tale
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AS the plane approaches Beirut I think nervously, "What will it be like?" Will I even recognize the glamorous, cosmopolitan city I remember from 25 years  before? It is 10 o'clock at night. I look down and see the lights of the corniche snaking around the edge of the city, but something's wrong: Beirut isn't bright enough. As we dip lower toward the southern suburbs I discover the  reason for this. Entire city blocks have been leveled; others contain only a couple of dark and abandoned high-rises and few lighted hovels. I am not as shocked by this as perhaps I should be. After all, New York has contrived similar effects in Harlem and the Bronx without the excuse of 15 years of savage, internecine conflict, involving a bewildering variety of Muslim and Christian factions, murderous private militias and the armies of Syria and Israel. (It ended only in 1990.) 

      The road into town passes the pine forest planted in the 17th century by the Emir Fakhr ad-Din II (one of Lebanon's great national heroes). It has been blasted to pieces, but a large sign announces that it is being restored. Everywhere there are signs of reconstruction. It becomes difficult to distinguish the half-ruined buildings from the half-built. As we descend into the old downtown area, however, there is no such ambiguity: everything is wrecked, burned out and pitted with holes. In some cases masonry has been so extensively remodeled it resembles rotting cheese. At the bottom of a hill my driver makes an expansive gesture, laughs ruefully and announes, "The city center!" All I can see is a vast, dark, empty space bordered by ruins.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: CONEY ISLAND;Ms. Szczepanski, Flame Swallower, to Wed the Great Fredini
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When Kiva Szczepanski first laid eyes on Fred Kahl in the summer of '92, she  and a date were in the audience at Sideshows by the Seashore in Coney Island, watching Mr. Kahl -- a.k.a. the Human Blockhead -- drive nails up his nose with  a hammer. 

     Ms. Szczepanski's disgusted companion insisted that they leave immediately. But she remembers taking one last look at Mr. Kahl, metal flaring his nostrils as he confidently goofed on the audience, and thinking, "Hmmm. I could really like this guy."

PLAYING IN THE NEIGHBORHOOD: HARLEM;The Rhythmic Gestures of an African Past
Professor Warren's Disciple
nytimes_articles.abstract:
I first saw Austin Warren one night in the winter of 1953, when he was a visiting professor at N.Y.U., teaching a class for graduate students in the novels of Henry James. The room was a mess of ground-out cigarette butts. Students had heard Warren was good, and every seat was taken 15 minutes before class started. Some students were sitting on the floor. Warren arrived about 15 minutes late, wearing a black suit and vest. He went to the front and faced us and said in a strong voice, while stabbing at the floor with his cane: "Henry James lived here. He walked these streets in Washington Square." As Warren spoke, he smiled with a sort of fierce exultation, and the class was under way.  He read some sentences aloud from James's novel "Washington Square," and told us to notice the adverbs. Warren didn't mangle a sentence with analysis, but rather urged us to "get it," and savor the richness of Jamesian implication. He seemed  to introduce us, at a very high level, to what might be called literary experience, and in his class it seemed as important or meaningful as religious experience. 

     The 50's were a literary time, dominated by the Talmudic practices of the New Criticism. In those days, a critic's job of interpretation included judgment of  value and could have the force of revelation. I thought the New Critics were giants. I still think so.

SOAPBOX;Dutch Treat for Polluters
nytimes_articles.abstract:
SHORTLY after she took office, Governor Whitman declared that New Jersey was  "open for business." And by that, it turned out, she largely meant finding ways to reduce the regulatory burden placed on businesses by environmental laws  that are among the nation's toughest. 

     But while meeting the needs of industry, she also needed to find a way to hone her own image as an environmentalist. Her administration's search for a way to get environmental regulators off the backs of business uncovered what seemed  to be the ideal model: the Netherlands' Green Plan, which embodies a system of cooperation between regulators and industry.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: SUNSET PARK/BAY RIDGE;Developer Seeks An Alternative To Theater Plan
nytimes_articles.abstract:
After being bombarded by local opposition, the developer who proposed an eight-screen movie theater has agreed to shelve the project indefinitely and to  discuss alternatives. 

     But about the only guarantee residents have gotten is that the developer, Tree Company, is prepared to talk about building something else at Bay Ridge and Narrows Avenues.  When plans to develop a United Artists multiplex there were made public, residents wrote letters and came out in large numbers to protest, saying it would draw too many cars.

INSIDE
OUT OF ORDER;A Man for All Seasons (Except Spring)
VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;A Gift From Mom In Her New Career
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN I was finishing college in the late 70's, the women's liberation movement was in full gear, and many male-centered careers were genuinely beginning to open up to women. My generation had the option of entering the world of business, law, medicine or, indeed, the more traditional women's fields.  Always the opportunist, I chose business. 

     For many years, I worked in the field of systems management, developing and testing computer systems.  It was fascinating, and I never contemplated a career change. I often thought, in fact, about how lucky I was to be living in a time when educated women could become a part of the work force whose decisions directly affected the real world. But 10 years ago, when my husband and I decided to have a family, my career took a strange twist.

Alaska: The Chilly Charms of Point Barrow
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Because most of the passenger compartment had been sealed off as a cargo hold for supplies going to Prudhoe Bay, the handful of passengers disembarking at Barrow, Alaska - two Eskimo families, a package-tour group of uneasy Midwesterners wearing "A Day at the Top" buttons on their sweaters and us - had  to leave the plane through the exit ramp near the tail. At the bottom, we took our first good look around. 

      "What are we doing here," my wife, Charlotte, murmured bleadly. I detected an uncharacteristic slump to her shoulders.

nytimes_articles.abstract: BETWEEN SISTERS By Nina Vida. 265 pp. New York: Crown Publishers. $23.     THESE days, it sometimes seems as if losers and victims have staged a coup within the walled city of American fiction. Brandishing their canes, wearing their bruises like dark cockades, they ring our literary battlements. Modern fiction used to be a relaxed kind of place, where friends gave each other high fives, where liberals and neocons sometimes pooled groceries, where the condescending honk of the patrician could be heard alongside the earnest dialect of political correctness. Nowadays, the streets seem a little empty, except for  the ragged new warriors: that privileged minority of victims, the inner cabal of the maimed.

wiki_events.headline: Severe thunderstorms and a tornado in Bangladesh kill 600 people.

pages.title: Thunderstorm
pages.abstract: A thunderstorm, also known as an electrical storm or a lightning storm, is a storm characterized by the presence of lightning and thunder. Relatively weak thunderstorms are sometimes called thundershowers. Thunderstorms occur in cumulonimbus clouds. They are usually accompanied by strong winds and often produce heavy rain and sometimes snow, sleet, or hail, but some thunderstorms can produce little or no precipitation at all. Thunderstorms may line up in a series or become a rainband, known as a squall line. Strong or severe thunderstorms include some of the most dangerous weather phenomena, including large hail, strong winds, and tornadoes. Some of the most persistent severe thunderstorms, known as supercells, rotate as do cyclones. While most thunderstorms move with the mean wind flow through the layer of the troposphere that they occupy, vertical wind shear sometimes causes a deviation in their course at a right angle to the wind shear direction.

pages.title: Tornado
pages.abstract: A tornado, also known as a twister, is a rapidly rotating column of air that extends vertically from the surface of the Earth to the base of a cumulonimbus or cumulus cloud. Tornadoes are often visible in the form of a condensation funnel originating from the cloud base, with a cloud of rotating debris and dust close to the ground. Most tornadoes have wind speeds less than 180 kilometers per hour, are about 80 meters across, and travel several kilometers before dissipating. The most extreme tornadoes can attain wind speeds of more than 480 kilometers per hour (300 mph), can be more than 3 kilometers (2 mi) in diameter, and can stay on the ground for more than 100 km (62 mi).

pages.title: Bangladesh
pages.abstract: Bangladesh, officially the People's Republic of Bangladesh, is a country in South Asia. It is the eighth-most populous country in the world and among the most densely populated with a population of almost 176 million within an area of 148,460 square kilometres (57,320 sq mi). Bangladesh shares land borders with India to the north, west, and east, and Myanmar to the southeast. It has a coastline along the Bay of Bengal to its south and is separated from Bhutan and Nepal by the Siliguri Corridor, and from China by the Indian state of Sikkim to its north. Dhaka, the capital and largest city, is the nation's political, financial, and cultural centre, with its most affluent neighborhood Gulshan being among the most posh neighborhoods in South Asia. Chittagong is the second-largest city and the busiest port of the country.
